members.name: PrintOut
members.signature: PrintOut([From As Variant], [To As Variant], [Copies As Variant], [Preview As Variant], [ActivePrinter As Variant], [PrintToFile As Variant], [Collate As Variant], [PrToFileName As Variant])

doc.summary: Prints the object.

params.name: From
params.text: The number of the page at which to start printing. If this argument is omitted, printing starts at the beginning.

params.name: To
params.text: The number of the last page to print. If this argument is omitted, printing ends with the last page.

params.name: Copies
params.text: The number of copies to print. If this argument is omitted, one copy is printed.

params.name: Preview
params.text: True to have Microsoft Excel invoke print preview before printing the object. False (or omitted) to print the object immediately.

params.name: ActivePrinter
params.text: Sets the name of the active printer.

params.name: PrintToFile
params.text: True to print to a file. If _PrToFileName_ is not specified, Excel prompts the user to enter the name of the output file.

params.name: Collate
params.text: True to collate multiple copies.

params.name: PrToFileName
params.text: If _PrintToFile_ is set to True, this argument specifies the name of the file that you want to print to.

doc.summary: Shows a preview of the object as it would look when printed.

params.name: EnableChanges
params.text: Pass a Boolean value to specify if the user can change the margins and other page setup options available in print preview.

members.name: ScaleHeight
members.signature: ScaleHeight(Factor As Single, RelativeToOriginalSize As MsoTriState, [Scale As Variant])

doc.summary: Scales the height of the shape by a specified factor. For pictures and OLE objects, you can indicate whether you want to scale the shape relative to the original or the current size. Shapes other than pictures and OLE objects are always scaled relative to their current height.

params.name: Factor
params.text: Specifies the ratio between the height of the shape after you resize it and the current or original height. For example, to make a rectangle 50 percent larger, specify 1.5 for this argument.

params.name: RelativeToOriginalSize
params.text: msoTrue to scale the shape relative to its original size. msoFalse to scale it relative to its current size. You can specify msoTrue for this argument only if the specified shape is a picture or an OLE object.

params.name: Scale
params.text: One of the constants of MsoScaleFrom, which specifies which part of the shape retains its position when the shape is scaled.

members.name: ScaleWidth
members.signature: ScaleWidth(Factor As Single, RelativeToOriginalSize As MsoTriState, [Scale As Variant])

doc.summary: Scales the width of the shape by a specified factor. For pictures and OLE objects, you can indicate whether you want to scale the shape relative to the original or the current size. Shapes other than pictures and OLE objects are always scaled relative to their current width.

params.name: Factor
params.text: Specifies the ratio between the width of the shape after you resize it and the current or original width. For example, to make a rectangle 50 percent larger, specify 1.5 for this argument.

params.name: RelativeToOriginalSize
params.text: msoFalse to scale it relative to its current size. You can specify msoTrue for this argument only if the specified shape is a picture or an OLE object.

params.name: Scale
params.text: One of the constants of MsoScaleFrom, which specifies which part of the shape retains its position when the shape is scaled.

members.name: AddChart2
members.signature: AddChart2([Style As Variant], [XlChartType As Variant], [Left As Variant], [Top As Variant], [Width As Variant], [Height As Variant], [NewLayout As Variant]) As Shape

doc.summary: Adds a chart to the document. Returns a Shape object that represents a chart and adds it to the specified collection.

params.name: Style
params.text: The chart style. Use "-1" to get the default style for the chart type specified in XlChartType.

params.name: XlChartType
params.text: The type of chart.

params.name: Left
params.text: The position, in points, of the left edge of the chart, relative to the anchor.

params.name: Top
params.text: The position, in points, of the top edge of the chart, relative to the anchor.

params.name: Width
params.text: The width, in points, of the chart.

params.name: Height
params.text: The height, in points, of the chart.

params.name: NewLayout
params.text: If NewLayout is True, the chart is inserted by using the new dynamic formatting rules (Title is on, and Legend is on only if there are multiple series).

members.name: AddConnector
members.signature: AddConnector(Type As MsoConnectorType, BeginX As Single, BeginY As Single, EndX As Single, EndY As Single) As Shape

doc.summary: Creates a connector. Returns a Shape object that represents the new connector. When a connector is added, it's not connected to anything. Use the BeginConnect and EndConnect methods to attach the beginning and end of a connector to other shapes in the document.

params.name: Type
params.text: The connector type to add.

params.name: BeginX
params.text: The horizontal position (in points) of the connector's starting point relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

params.name: BeginY
params.text: The vertical position (in points) of the connector's starting point relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

params.name: EndX
params.text: The horizontal position (in points) of the connector's end point relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

params.name: EndY
params.text: The vertical position (in points) of the connector's end point relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

members.name: AddLabel
members.signature: AddLabel(Orientation As MsoTextOrientation, Left As Single, Top As Single, Width As Single, Height As Single) As Shape

doc.summary: Creates a label. Returns a Shape object that represents the new label.

params.name: Orientation
params.text: The text orientation within the label.

params.name: Left
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the label relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

params.name: Top
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the label relative to the top of the document.

params.name: Width
params.text: The width of the label, in points.

params.name: Height
params.text: The height of the label, in points.

members.name: AddLine
members.signature: AddLine(BeginX As Single, BeginY As Single, EndX As Single, EndY As Single) As Shape

doc.summary: As it applies to the Shapes object, returns a Shape object that represents the new line on a worksheet.

params.name: BeginX
params.text: The position (in points) of the line's starting point relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

params.name: BeginY
params.text: The position (in points) of the line's starting point relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

params.name: EndX
params.text: The position (in points) of the line's end point relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

params.name: EndY
params.text: The position (in points) of the line's end point relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

members.name: AddPicture
members.signature: AddPicture(Filename As String, LinkToFile As MsoTriState, SaveWithDocument As MsoTriState, Left As Single, Top As Single, Width As Single, Height As Single) As Shape

doc.summary: Creates a picture from an existing file. Returns a Shape object that represents the new picture.

params.name: Filename
params.text: The file from which the picture is to be created.

params.name: LinkToFile
params.text: The file to link to. Use msoFalse to make the picture an independent copy of the file. Use msoTrue to link the picture to the file from which it was created.

params.name: SaveWithDocument
params.text: To save the picture with the document. Use msoFalse to store only the link information in the document. Use msoTrue to save the linked picture with the document into which it's inserted. This argument must be msoTrue if _LinkToFile_ is msoFalse.

params.name: Left
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the picture relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

params.name: Top
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the picture relative to the top of the document.

params.name: Width
params.text: The width of the picture, in points (enter -1 to retain the width of the existing file).

params.name: Height
params.text: The height of the picture, in points (enter -1 to retain the height of the existing file).

members.name: AddShape
members.signature: AddShape(Type As MsoAutoShapeType, Left As Single, Top As Single, Width As Single, Height As Single) As Shape

doc.summary: Returns a Shape object that represents the new AutoShape on a worksheet.

params.text: Specifies the type of AutoShape to create.

params.name: Left
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the AutoShape's bounding box relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

params.name: Top
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the AutoShape's bounding box relative to the top of the document.

params.name: Width
params.text: The width of the AutoShape's bounding box, in points.

params.name: Height
params.text: The height of the AutoShape's bounding box, in points.

params.name: Top

params.name: Width
params.text: The width of the text box, in points.

params.name: Height
params.text: The height of the text box, in points.

params.name: Index
params.text: The name or index number for the object.

members.name: Add3DModel
members.signature: Add3DModel(Filename As String, [LinkToFile As Variant], [SaveWithDocument As Variant], [Left As Variant], [Top As Variant], [Width As Variant], [Height As Variant]) As Shape

doc.summary: Creates a 3D model from an existing file. Returns a Shape object that represents the new 3D model.

params.name: Filename
params.text: The file from which the 3D model is to be created.

params.name: LinkToFile
params.text: Determines whether the 3D model will be linked to the file from which it was created.

params.name: SaveWithDocument
params.text: Determines whether the linked 3D model will be saved with the document into which it is inserted.

params.name: Left
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the 3D model relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

params.name: Top
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the 3D model relative to the top of the document.

params.name: Width
params.text: The width of the 3D model, in points (enter -1 to auto-calculate a width based on the 3D model dimensions).

params.name: Height
params.text: The height of the 3D model, in points (enter -1 to auto-calculate a height based on the 3D model dimensions).

members.name: AddCallout
members.signature: AddCallout(Type As MsoCalloutType, Left As Single, Top As Single, Width As Single, Height As Single) As Shape

doc.summary: Creates a borderless line callout. Returns a Shape object that represents the new callout.

params.name: Type
params.text: The type of callout line.

params.name: Left
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the callout's bounding box relative to the upper-left corner of the document.

params.name: Top
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the callout's bounding box relative to the top of the document.

params.name: Width
params.text: The width of the callout's bounding box, in points.

params.name: Height
params.text: The height of the callout's bounding box, in points.

doc.summary: Returns a Shape object that represents a Bzier curve on a worksheet.

params.name: SafeArrayOfPoints
params.text: An array of coordinate pairs that specifies the vertices and control points of the curve. The first point that you specify is the starting vertex, and the next two points are control points for the first Bzier segment. Then, for each additional segment of the curve, you specify a vertex and two control points. The last point that you specify is the ending vertex for the curve. Note that you must always specify 3n + 1 points, where n is the number of segments in the curve.

members.name: AddFormControl
members.signature: AddFormControl(Type As XlFormControl, Left As Long, Top As Long, Width As Long, Height As Long) As Shape

doc.summary: Creates a Microsoft Excel control. Returns a Shape object that represents the new control.

params.name: Type
params.text: The Microsoft Excel control type. You cannot create an edit box on a worksheet.

params.name: Left
params.text: The initial coordinates of the new object (in points) relative to the upper-left corner of cell A1 on a worksheet or to the upper-left corner of a chart.

params.name: Top
params.text: The initial coordinates of the new object (in points) relative to the top of row 1 on a worksheet, or to the top of the chart area on a chart.

params.name: Width
params.text: The initial size of the new object, in points.

params.name: Height
params.text: The initial size of the new object, in points.

members.name: AddOLEObject
members.signature: AddOLEObject([ClassType As Variant], [Filename As Variant], [Link As Variant], [DisplayAsIcon As Variant], [IconFileName As Variant], [IconIndex As Variant], [IconLabel As Variant], [Left As Variant], [Top As Variant], [Width As Variant], [Height As Variant]) As Shape

doc.summary: Creates an OLE object. Returns a Shape object that represents the new OLE object.

params.name: ClassType
params.text: A string that contains the programmatic identifier for the object to be created. You must specify either _ClassType_ or _FileName_. If _ClassType_ is specified, _FileName_ and _Link_ are ignored.

params.name: Filename
params.text: The file from which the object is to be created. If the path isn't specified, the current working folder is used. You must specify either the _ClassType_ or _FileName_ argument for the object, but not both.

params.name: Link
params.text: True to link the OLE object to the file from which it was created. False to make the OLE object an independent copy of the file. If you specified a value for _ClassType_, this argument must be False. The default value is False.

params.name: DisplayAsIcon
params.text: True to display the OLE object as an icon. The default value is False.

params.name: IconFileName
params.text: The file that contains the icon to be displayed.

params.name: IconIndex
params.text: The index of the icon within _IconFileName_. The order of icons in the specified file corresponds to the order in which the icons appear in the Change Icon dialog box (accessed from the Object dialog box when the Display as icon check box is selected). The first icon in the file has the index number 0 (zero). If an icon with the given index number doesn't exist in _IconFileName_, the icon with the index number 1 (the second icon in the file) is used. The default value is 0 (zero).

params.name: IconLabel
params.text: A label (caption) to be displayed beneath the icon.

params.name: Left
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the new object relative to the upper-left corner of the document. The default value is 0 (zero).

params.name: Top
params.text: The position (in points) of the upper-left corner of the new object relative to the top of the document. The default value is 0 (zero).

params.name: Width
params.text: The initial dimensions of the OLE object, in points.

params.name: Height
params.text: The initial dimensions of the OLE object, in points.

members.name: AddPicture2

doc.summary: Creates a picture from an existing file. Returns a Shape object that represents the new picture.

params.name: Filename
params.text: The file from which the OLE object is to be created.

params.name: LinkToFile

params.name: SaveWithDocument
params.text: Determines whether the linked picture will be saved with the document into which it is inserted. This argument must be msoTrue if _LinkToFile_ is msoFalse.

params.name: Left
params.text: The position, measured in points, of the left edge of the picture relative to the left edge of the worksheet.

params.name: Top
params.text: The position, measured in points, of the top edge of the picture relative to the top edge of the worksheet.

params.name: Width
params.text: The width of the picture, measured in points.

params.name: Height
params.text: The height of the picture, measured in points.

params.name: Compress
params.text: Determines whether the picture should be compressed when inserted.

doc.summary: Displays a cached document, if it's already been downloaded. Otherwise, this method resolves the hyperlink, downloads the target document, and displays the document in the appropriate application.

params.name: NewWindow
params.text: True to display the target application in a new window. The default value is False.

params.text: Not used. Reserved for future use.

params.name: ExtraInfo
params.text: A String or byte array that specifies additional information for HTTP to use to resolve the hyperlink. For example, you can use _ExtraInfo_ to specify the coordinates of an image map, the contents of a form, or a FAT file name.

params.name: Method
params.text: Specifies the way _ExtraInfo_ is attached. Can be one of the MsoExtraInfoMethod constants.

params.name: HeaderInfo
params.text: A String that specifies header information for the HTTP request. The default value is an empty string.

True if data validation displays a drop-down list that contains acceptable values. Read/write Boolean.
Returns or sets the data validation input message. Read/write String.
Returns or sets the title of the data-validation input dialog box. Read/write String. Limited to 32 characters.
Returns a Long value that represents the operator for the data validation. See XlFormatConditionOperator.
Returns the parent object for the specified object. Read-only.
True if the data validation error message will be displayed whenever the user enters invalid data. Read/write Boolean.
True if the data validation input message will be displayed whenever the user selects a cell in the data validation range. Read/write Boolean.
Returns a Long value containing an XlDVType constant that represents the data type validation for a range.
Returns a Boolean value that indicates if all the validation criteria are met (that is, if the range contains valid data).

members.name: Add
members.signature: Add(Type As XlDVType, [AlertStyle As Variant], [Operator As Variant], [Formula1 As Variant], [Formula2 As Variant])

doc.summary: Adds data validation to the specified range.

params.name: Type
params.text: The validation type.

params.name: AlertStyle
params.text: The validation alert style. Can be one of the following XlDVAlertStyle constants: xlValidAlertInformation, xlValidAlertStop, or xlValidAlertWarning.

params.name: Operator
params.text: The data validation operator. Can be one of the following XlFormatConditionOperator constants: xlBetween, xlEqual, xlGreater, xlGreaterEqual, xlLess, xlLessEqual, xlNotBetween, or xlNotEqual.

params.name: Formula1
params.text: The first part of the data validation equation. Value must not exceed 255 characters.

params.name: Formula2
params.text: The second part of the data validation equation when _Operator_ is xlBetween or xlNotBetween (otherwise, this argument is ignored).

doc.summary: Deletes the object.

doc.summary: Returns a 32-bit integer that indicates the application in which this object was created. Read-only Long.

Returns the parent object for the specified object. Read-only.

members.name: HLookup
members.signature: HLookup(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant, Arg3 As Variant, [Arg4 As Variant]) As Variant

doc.summary: Searches for a value in the top row of a table or an array of values, and then returns a value in the same column from a row that you specify in the table or array. Use HLookup when your comparison values are located in a row across the top of a table of data, and you want to look down a specified number of rows. Use VLookup when your comparison values are located in a column to the left of the data that you want to find.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Lookup_value - the value to be found in the first row of the table. Lookup_value can be a value, a reference, or a text string.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Table_array - a table of information in which data is looked up. Use a reference to a range or a range name.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Row_index_num - the row number in table_array from which the matching value will be returned. A row_index_num of 1 returns the first row value in table_array, a row_index_num of 2 returns the second row value in table_array, and so on. If row_index_num is less than 1, HLookup returns the #VALUE! error value; if row_index_num is greater than the number of rows on table_array, HLookup returns the #REF! error value.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Range_lookup - a logical value that specifies whether you want HLookup to find an exact match or an approximate match. If True or omitted, an approximate match is returned. In other words, if an exact match is not found, the next largest value that is less than lookup_value is returned. If False, HLookup will find an exact match. If one is not found, the error value #N/A is returned.

members.name: IfError
members.signature: IfError(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant) As Variant

doc.summary: Returns a value that you specify if a formula evaluates to an error; otherwise, returns the result of the formula. Use the IfError function to trap and handle errors in a formula.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Value - the argument that is checked for an error.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Value_if_error - the value to return if the formula evaluates to an error. The following error types are evaluated: #N/A, #VALUE!, #REF!, #DIV/0!, #NUM!, #NAME?, or #NULL!.

members.name: Index
members.signature: Index(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Double, [Arg3 As Variant], [Arg4 As Variant]) As Variant

doc.summary: Returns a value or the reference to a value from within a table or range. There are two forms of the Index function: the array form and the reference form.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Array or Reference - a range of cells or an array constant. For references, it is the reference to one or more cell ranges.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Row_num - selects the row in array from which to return a value. If row_num is omitted, column_num is required. For references, the number of the row in reference from which to return a reference.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Column_num - selects the column in array from which to return a value. If column_num is omitted, row_num is required. For reference, the number of the column in reference from which to return a reference.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Area_num - only used when returning references. Selects a range in reference from which to return the intersection of row_num and column_num. The first area selected or entered is numbered 1, the second is 2, and so on. If area_num is omitted, Index uses area 1.

members.name: Large
members.signature: Large(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Double) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the k-th largest value in a data set. Use this function to select a value based on its relative standing. For example, you can use Large to return the highest, runner-up, or third-place score.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Array - the array or range of data for which you want to determine the k-th largest value.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: K - the position (from the largest) in the array or cell range of data to return.

members.name: Lookup
members.signature: Lookup(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant, [Arg3 As Variant]) As Variant

doc.summary: Returns a value either from a one-row or one-column range or from an array. The Lookup function has two syntax forms: the vector form and the array form.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Lookup_value - A value that Lookup searches for in the first vector. Lookup_value can be a number, text, a logical value, or a name or reference that refers to a value.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Lookup_vector or Array - In vector form, a range that contains only one row or one column. The values in lookup_vector can be text, numbers, or logical values. In array form, a range of cells that contains text, numbers, or logical values that you want to compare with lookup_value.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Result_vector - Only used with the vector form. A range that contains only one row or column. It must be the same size as lookup_vector.

members.name: Match
members.signature: Match(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant, [Arg3 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the relative position of an item in an array that matches a specified value in a specified order. Use Match instead of one of the Lookup functions when you need the position of an item in a range instead of the item itself.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Lookup_value: the value that you use to find the value that you want in a table.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Lookup_array: a contiguous range of cells containing possible lookup values. Lookup_array must be an array or an array reference.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Match_type: the number -1, 0, or 1. Match_type specifies how Microsoft Excel matches lookup_value with values in lookup_array.

members.name: StDev
members.signature: StDev(Arg1 As Variant, [Arg2 As Variant], [Arg3 As Variant], [Arg4 As Variant], [Arg5 As Variant], [Arg6 As Variant], [Arg7 As Variant], [Arg8 As Variant], [Arg9 As Variant], [Arg10 As Variant], [Arg11 As Variant], [Arg12 As Variant], [Arg13 As Variant], [Arg14 As Variant], [Arg15 As Variant], [Arg16 As Variant], [Arg17 As Variant], [Arg18 As Variant], [Arg19 As Variant], [Arg20 As Variant], [Arg21 As Variant], [Arg22 As Variant], [Arg23 As Variant], [Arg24 As Variant], [Arg25 As Variant], [Arg26 As Variant], [Arg27 As Variant], [Arg28 As Variant], [Arg29 As Variant], [Arg30 As Variant]) As Double

members.name: Substitute
members.signature: Substitute(Arg1 As String, Arg2 As String, Arg3 As String, [Arg4 As Variant]) As String

doc.summary: Substitutes new_text for old_text in a text string. Use Substitute when you want to replace specific text in a text string; use Replace when you want to replace any text that occurs in a specific location in a text string.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Text - the text or the reference to a cell containing text for which you want to substitute characters.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Old_text - the text that you want to replace.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: New_text - the text that you want to replace old_text with.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Instance_num - specifies which occurrence of old_text you want to replace with new_text. If you specify instance_num, only that instance of old_text is replaced. Otherwise, every occurrence of old_text in text is changed to new_text.

doc.summary: Returns a vertical range of cells as a horizontal range, or vice versa. Transpose must be entered as an array formula in a range that has the same number of rows and columns, respectively, as an array has columns and rows. Use Transpose to shift the vertical and horizontal orientation of an array on a worksheet.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Array - an array or range of cells on a worksheet that you want to transpose. The transpose of an array is created by using the first row of the array as the first column of the new array, the second row of the array as the second column of the new array, and so on.

doc.summary: Removes all spaces from text except for single spaces between words. Use Trim on text that you have received from another application that may have irregular spacing.

params.name: Arg1

members.name: VLookup
members.signature: VLookup(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant, Arg3 As Variant, [Arg4 As Variant]) As Variant

doc.summary: Searches for a value in the first column of a table array and returns a value in the same row from another column in the table array.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Lookup_value - the value to search in the first column of the table array. Lookup_value can be a value or a reference. If lookup_value is smaller than the smallest value in the first column of table_array, VLookup returns the #N/A error value.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Table_array - two or more columns of data. Use a reference to a range or a range name. The values in the first column of table_array are the values searched by lookup_value. These values can be text, numbers, or logical values. Uppercase and lowercase text are equivalent.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Col_index_num - the column number in table_array from which the matching value must be returned. A col_index_num of 1 returns the value in the first column in table_array; a col_index_num of 2 returns the value in the second column in table_array, and so on.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Range_lookup - a logical value that specifies whether you want the VLookup method to find an exact match or an approximate match.

0 or omittedIgnore nested SUBTOTAL and AGGREGATE functions
1Ignore hidden rows, nested SUBTOTAL and AGGREGATE functions

members.name: DAverage
members.signature: DAverage(Arg1 As Range, Arg2 As Variant, Arg3 As Variant) As Double

doc.summary: Averages the values in a column of a list or database that match conditions that you specify.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: The range of cells that makes up the list or database. A database is a list of related data in which rows of related information are records, and columns of data are fields. The first row of the list contains labels for each column.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Indicates which column is used in the function. Enter the column label enclosed between double quotation marks, such as "Age" or "Yield," or a number (without quotation marks) that represents the position of the column within the list: 1 for the first column, 2 for the second column, and so on.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: The range of cells that contains the conditions that you specify. Use any range for the criteria argument, as long as it includes at least one column label and at least one cell below the column label in which you specify a condition for the column.

members.name: Db

params.text: Salvage - the value at the end of the depreciation (sometimes called the salvage value of the asset).

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Life - the number of periods over which the asset is being depreciated (sometimes called the useful life of the asset).

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Period - the period for which you want to calculate the depreciation. Period must use the same units as life.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Month - the number of months in the first year. If month is omitted, it is assumed to be 12.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Cost - the initial cost of the asset.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Salvage - the value at the end of the depreciation (sometimes called the salvage value of the asset). This value can be 0.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Life - the number of periods over which the asset is being depreciated (sometimes called the useful life of the asset).

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Period - the period for which you want to calculate the depreciation. Period must use the same units as life.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Factor - the rate at which the balance declines. If factor is omitted, it is assumed to be 2 (the double-declining balance method).

members.name: Dec2Bin
members.signature: Dec2Bin(Arg1 As Variant, [Arg2 As Variant]) As String

doc.summary: Converts a decimal number to binary.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the decimal integer that you want to convert. If number is negative, valid place values are ignored and Dec2Bin returns a 10-character (10-bit) binary number in which the most significant bit is the sign bit. The remaining 9 bits are magnitude bits. Negative numbers are represented using two's-complement notation.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Places - the number of characters to use. If places is omitted, Dec2Bin uses the minimum number of characters necessary. Places is useful for padding the return value with leading 0s (zeros).

members.name: Dec2Hex
members.signature: Dec2Hex(Arg1 As Variant, [Arg2 As Variant]) As String

doc.summary: Converts a decimal number to hexadecimal.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the decimal integer that you want to convert. If number is negative, places is ignored and Dec2Hex returns a 10-character (40-bit) hexadecimal number in which the most significant bit is the sign bit. The remaining 39 bits are magnitude bits. Negative numbers are represented using two's-complement notation.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Places - the number of characters to use. If places is omitted, Dec2Hex uses the minimum number of characters necessary. Places is useful for padding the return value with leading 0s (zeros).

members.name: Dec2Oct
members.signature: Dec2Oct(Arg1 As Variant, [Arg2 As Variant]) As String

doc.summary: Converts a decimal number to octal.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the decimal integer that you want to convert. If number is negative, places is ignored and Dec2Oct returns a 10-character (30-bit) octal number in which the most significant bit is the sign bit. The remaining 29 bits are magnitude bits. Negative numbers are represented using two's-complement notation.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Places - the number of characters to use. If places is omitted, Dec2Oct uses the minimum number of characters necessary. Places is useful for padding the return value with leading 0s (zeros).

params.text: Criteria - the range of cells that contains the conditions that you specify. Use any range for the criteria argument, as long as it includes at least one column label and at least one cell below the column label in which you specify a condition for the column.

Enter the column label enclosed between double quotation marks, such as "Age" or "Yield," or a number (without quotation marks) that represents the position of the column within the list: 1 for the first column, 2 for the second column, and so on.

members.name: Dollar

doc.summary: The function described in this Help topic converts a number to text format and applies a currency symbol. The name of the function (and the symbol that it applies) depends upon your language settings.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - a number, a reference to a cell containing a number, or a formula that evaluates to a number.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Decimals - the number of digits to the right of the decimal point. If decimals is negative, number is rounded to the left of the decimal point. If you omit decimals, it is assumed to be 2.

members.name: DollarDe

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Fraction - the integer to use in the denominator of the fraction.

members.name: DollarFr
members.signature: DollarFr(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant) As Double

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Decimal_dollar - a decimal number.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Fraction - the integer to use in the denominator of a fraction.

members.name: Duration
members.signature: Duration(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant, Arg3 As Variant, Arg4 As Variant, Arg5 As Variant, [Arg6 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the Macauley duration for an assumed par value of $100. Duration is defined as the weighted average of the present value of the cash flows and is used as a measure of a bond price's response to changes in yield.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Settlement - the security's settlement date. The security settlement date is the date after the issue date when the security is traded to the buyer.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Maturity - the security's maturity date. The maturity date is the date when the security expires.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Coupon - the security's annual coupon rate.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Yld - the security's annual yield.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Frequency - the number of coupon payments per year. For annual payments, frequency = 1; for semiannual, frequency = 2; for quarterly, frequency = 4.

params.name: Arg6
params.text: Basis - the type of day count basis to use.

members.name: EDate
members.signature: EDate(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the serial number that represents the date that is the indicated number of months before or after a specified date (the start_date). Use EDate to calculate maturity dates or due dates that fall on the same day of the month as the date of issue.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Start_date - a date that represents the start date. Dates should be entered by using the DATE function, or as results of other formulas or functions. For example, use DATE(2008,5,23) for the 23rd day of May, 2008. Problems can occur if dates are entered as text.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Months - the number of months before or after start_date. A positive value for months yields a future date; a negative value yields a past date.

members.name: Effect
members.signature: Effect(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the effective annual interest rate, given the nominal annual interest rate and the number of compounding periods per year.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Nominal_rate - the nominal interest rate.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Npery - the number of compounding periods per year.

doc.summary: URL encodes the argument.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Text to be encoded.

members.name: EoMonth
members.signature: EoMonth(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the serial number for the last day of the month that is the indicated number of months before or after start_date. Use EoMonth to calculate maturity dates or due dates that fall on the last day of the month.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Start_date - a date that represents the starting date. Dates should be entered by using the DATE function, or as results of other formulas or functions. For example, use DATE(2008,5,23) for the 23rd day of May, 2008. Problems can occur if dates are entered as text.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Months - the number of months before or after start_date. A positive value for months yields a future date; a negative value yields a past date.

members.name: Erf
members.signature: Erf(Arg1 As Variant, [Arg2 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the error function integrated between lower_limit and upper_limit.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Lower_limit - the lower bound for integrating Erf.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Upper_limit - the upper bound for integrating Erf. If omitted, Erf integrates between zero and lower_limit.

doc.summary: Returns the error function integrated between zero and lower_limit.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Lower_limit - the lower bound for integrating Erf.

doc.summary: Returns the complementary Erf function integrated between the specified parameter and infinity.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: The first argument.

doc.summary: Returns the complementary error function integrated between the specified value and infinity.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: The value to compute against.

doc.summary: Returns number rounded up to the nearest even integer. Use this function for processing items that come in twos. For example, a packing crate accepts rows of one or two items. The crate is full when the number of items, rounded up to the nearest two, matches the crate's capacity.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the value to round.

members.name: Expon_Dist
members.signature: Expon_Dist(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Boolean) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the exponential distribution. Use Expon_Dist to model the time between events, such as how long an automated bank teller takes to deliver cash. For example, you can use Expon_Dist to determine the probability that the process takes at most 1 minute.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: X - the value of the function.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Lambda - the parameter value.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Cumulative - a logical value that indicates which form of the exponential function to provide. If cumulative is True, EXPONDIST returns the cumulative distribution function; if False, it returns the probability density function.

members.name: ExponDist
members.signature: ExponDist(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Boolean) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the exponential distribution. Use ExponDist to model the time between events, such as how long an automated bank teller takes to deliver cash. For example, you can use ExponDist to determine the probability that the process takes at most 1 minute.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: X - the value of the function.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Lambda - the parameter value.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Cumulative - a logical value that indicates which form of the exponential function to provide. If cumulative is True, ExponDist returns the cumulative distribution function; if False, it returns the probability density function.

members.name: F_Dist
members.signature: F_Dist(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double, Arg4 As Boolean) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the F probability distribution.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: X - The value at which to evaluate the function.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Deg_freedom1 - The numerator degrees of freedom.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Deg_freedom2 - The denominator degrees of freedom.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Cumulative - A logical value that determines the form of the function. If cumulative is True, F_DIST returns the cumulative distribution function; if False, it returns the probability density function.

members.name: F_Dist_RT
members.signature: F_Dist_RT(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the right-tailed F probability distribution. Use this function to determine whether two data sets have different degrees of diversity. For example, you can examine the test scores of men and women entering high school and determine if the variability in the females is different from that found in the males.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: X - the value at which to evaluate the function.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Degrees_freedom1 - the numerator degrees of freedom.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Degrees_freedom2 - the denominator degrees of freedom.

members.name: F_Inv
members.signature: F_Inv(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the inverse of the F probability distribution.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Probability - A probability associated with the F cumulative distribution.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Deg_freedom1 - The numerator degrees of freedom.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Deg_freedom2 - The denominator degrees of freedom.

members.name: F_Inv_RT
members.signature: F_Inv_RT(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the inverse of the right-tailed F probability distribution. If p = F_DIST_RT(x,...), then F_INV_RT(p,...) = x.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Probability - a probability associated with the F cumulative distribution.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Degrees_freedom1 - the numerator degrees of freedom.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Degrees_freedom2 - the denominator degrees of freedom.

members.name: F_Test
members.signature: F_Test(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the result of an F-test. An F-test returns the two-tailed probability that the variances in array1 and array2 are not significantly different. Use this function to determine whether two samples have different variances. For example, given test scores from public and private schools, you can test whether these schools have different levels of test score diversity.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Array1 - the first array or range of data.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Array2 - the second array or range of data.

doc.summary: Returns the factorial of a number. The factorial of a number is equal to 1\2\3\...\ number.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the nonnegative number that you want the factorial of. If number is not an integer, it is truncated.

doc.summary: Returns the double factorial of a number.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the value for which to return the double factorial. If number is not an integer, it is truncated.

doc.summary: Returns the Fisher transformation at x. This transformation produces a function that is normally distributed rather than skewed. Use this function to perform hypothesis testing on the correlation coefficient.

members.name: Forecast_ETS
members.signature: Forecast_ETS(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Variant, Arg3 As Variant, [Arg4 As Variant], [Arg5 As Variant], [Arg6 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Calculates or predicts a future value based on existing (historical) values by using the AAA version of the Exponential Smoothing (ETS) algorithm.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Target Date: the data point for which you want to predict a value. Target date can be date/time or numeric. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Values: the historical values, for which you want to forecast the next points.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Timeline: the independent array or range of dates or numeric data. The values in the timeline must have a consistent step between them and can't be zero. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Seasonality: A numeric value. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Data completions: Although the timeline requires a constant step between data points, Forecast_ETS supports up to 30% missing data, and automatically adjusts for it. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg6
params.text: Aggregation: Although the timeline requires a constant step between data points, Forecast_ETS aggregates multiple points that have the same time stamp. See Remarks.

members.name: Forecast_ETS_ConfInt
members.signature: Forecast_ETS_ConfInt(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Variant, Arg3 As Variant, [Arg4 As Variant], [Arg5 As Variant], [Arg6 As Variant], [Arg7 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns a confidence interval for the forecast value at the specified target date.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Target Date: the data point for which you want to predict a value. Target date can be date/time or numeric. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Values: the historical values, for which you want to forecast the next points.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Timeline: the independent array or range of dates or numeric data. The values in the timeline must have a consistent step between them and can't be zero. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Confidence level: A numerical value between 0 and 1 (exclusive), indicating a confidence level for the calculated confidence interval. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Seasonality: A numeric value. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg6
params.text: Data completions: Although the timeline requires a constant step between data points, Forecast_ETS_ConfInt supports up to 30% missing data, and automatically adjusts for it. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg7
params.text: Aggregation: Although the timeline requires a constant step between data points, Forecast_ETS_ConfInt aggregates multiple points that have the same time stamp. See Remarks.

members.name: Forecast_ETS_Seasonality
members.signature: Forecast_ETS_Seasonality(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant, [Arg3 As Variant], [Arg4 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the length of the repetitive pattern that Excel detects for the specified time series.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Values: the historical values, for which you want to forecast the next points.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Timeline: the independent array or range of dates or numeric data. The values in the timeline must have a consistent step between them and can't be zero. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Data completions: Although the timeline requires a constant step between data points, Forecast_ETS_Seasonality supports up to 30% missing data, and automatically adjusts for it. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Aggregation: Although the timeline requires a constant step between data points, Forecast_ETS_Seasonality aggregates multiple points that have the same time stamp. See Remarks.

members.name: Forecast_ETS_STAT
members.signature: Forecast_ETS_STAT(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant, Arg3 As Double, [Arg4 As Variant], [Arg5 As Variant], [Arg6 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns a statistical value as a result of time series forecasting.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Values: the historical values, for which you want to forecast the next points.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Timeline: the independent array or range of dates or numeric data. The values in the timeline must have a consistent step between them and can't be zero. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Statistic_type: A numeric value between 1 and 8, indicating which statistic will be returned for the calculated forecast.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Confidence level: A numerical value between 0 and 1 (exclusive), indicating a confidence level for the calculated confidence interval. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Data completions: Although the timeline requires a constant step between data points, Forecast_ETS_STAT supports up to 30% missing data, and automatically adjusts for it. See Remarks.

params.name: Arg6
params.text: Aggregation: Although the timeline requires a constant step between data points, Forecast_ETS_STAT aggregates multiple points that have the same time stamp. See Remarks.

members.name: Forecast_Linear
members.signature: Forecast_Linear(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Variant, Arg3 As Variant) As Double

doc.summary: Calculates, or predicts, a future value by using existing values. The predicted value is a y-value for a given x-value. The known values are existing x-values and y-values, and the new value is predicted by using linear regression. Use this function to predict future sales, inventory requirements, or consumer trends.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: x - the data point for which you want to predict a value.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: known_y's - the dependent array or range of data.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: known_x's - the independent array or range of data.

members.name: Frequency
members.signature: Frequency(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant) As Variant

doc.summary: Calculates how often values occur within a range of values, and then returns a vertical array of numbers. For example, use Frequency to count the number of test scores that fall within ranges of scores. Because Frequency returns an array, it must be entered as an array formula.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Data_array - an array of or reference to a set of values for which you want to count frequencies. If data_array contains no values, Frequency returns an array of zeros.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Bins_array - an array of or reference to intervals into which you want to group the values in data_array. If bins_array contains no values, Frequency returns the number of elements in data_array.

members.name: FTest
members.signature: FTest(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the result of an F-test. An F-test returns the two-tailed probability that the variances in array1 and array2 are not significantly different. Use this function to determine whether two samples have different variances. For example, given test scores from public and private schools, you can test whether these schools have different levels of test score diversity.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Array1 - the first array or range of data.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Array2 - the second array or range of data.

members.name: Fv
members.signature: Fv(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double, [Arg4 As Variant], [Arg5 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the future value of an investment based on periodic, constant payments and a constant interest rate.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Rate - the interest rate per period.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Nper - the total number of payment periods in an annuity.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Pmt - the payment made each period; it cannot change over the life of the annuity. Typically, pmt contains principal and interest but no other fees or taxes. If pmt is omitted, you must include the pv argument.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Pv - the present value, or the lump-sum amount that a series of future payments is worth right now. If pv is omitted, it is assumed to be 0 (zero), and you must include the pmt argument.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Type - the number 0 or 1 and indicates when payments are due. If type is omitted, it is assumed to be 0.

members.name: FVSchedule
members.signature: FVSchedule(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the future value of an initial principal after applying a series of compound interest rates. Use FVSchedule to calculate the future value of an investment with a variable or adjustable rate.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Principal - the present value.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Schedule - an array of interest rates to apply.

doc.summary: Returns the gamma function value.

params.name: Arg1

members.name: Gamma_Dist
members.signature: Gamma_Dist(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double, Arg4 As Boolean) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the gamma distribution. Use this function to study variables that may have a skewed distribution. The gamma distribution is commonly used in queuing analysis.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: X - the value at which you want to evaluate the distribution.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Alpha - a parameter to the distribution.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Beta - a parameter to the distribution. If beta = 1, Gamma_Dist returns the standard gamma distribution.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Cumulative - a logical value that determines the form of the function. If cumulative is True, Gamma_Dist returns the cumulative distribution function; if False, it returns the probability density function.

members.name: Gamma_Inv

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Alpha - a parameter to the distribution.

params.name: Arg3

doc.summary: Returns 1 if number step; otherwise, returns 0 (zero). Use this function to filter a set of values. For example, by summing several GeStep functions, you calculate the count of values that exceed a threshold.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the value to test against step.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Step - the threshold value. If you omit a value for step, GeStep uses zero.

members.name: Growth
members.signature: Growth(Arg1 As Variant, [Arg2 As Variant], [Arg3 As Variant], [Arg4 As Variant]) As Variant

doc.summary: Calculates predicted exponential growth by using existing data. Growth returns the y-values for a series of new x-values that you specify by using existing x-values and y-values. You can also use the Growth worksheet function to fit an exponential curve to existing x-values and y-values.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Known_y's - the set of y-values that you already know in the relationship y = b*m^x.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Known_x's - an optional set of x-values that you may already know in the relationship y = b*m^x.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: New_x's - new x-values for which you want Growth to return corresponding y-values.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Const - a logical value specifying whether to force the constant b to equal 1.

doc.summary: Returns the harmonic mean of a data set. The harmonic mean is the reciprocal of the arithmetic mean of reciprocals.

members.name: Hex2Bin
members.signature: Hex2Bin(Arg1 As Variant, [Arg2 As Variant]) As String

doc.summary: Converts a hexadecimal number to binary.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the hexadecimal number that you want to convert. Number cannot contain more than 10 characters. The most significant bit of number is the sign bit (40th bit from the right). The remaining 9 bits are magnitude bits. Negative numbers are represented using two's-complement notation.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Places - the number of characters to use. If places is omitted, Hex2Bin uses the minimum number of characters necessary. Places is useful for padding the return value with leading 0s (zeros).

doc.summary: Converts a hexadecimal number to decimal.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the hexadecimal number that you want to convert. Number cannot contain more than 10 characters (40 bits). The most significant bit of number is the sign bit. The remaining 39 bits are magnitude bits. Negative numbers are represented using two's-complement notation.

members.name: Hex2Oct
members.signature: Hex2Oct(Arg1 As Variant, [Arg2 As Variant]) As String

doc.summary: Converts a hexadecimal number to octal.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the hexadecimal number that you want to convert. Number cannot contain more than 10 characters. The most significant bit of number is the sign bit. The remaining 39 bits are magnitude bits. Negative numbers are represented using two's-complement notation.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Places - the number of characters to use. If places is omitted, Hex2Oct uses the minimum number of characters necessary. Places is useful for padding the return value with leading 0s (zeros).

members.name: HypGeom_Dist
members.signature: HypGeom_Dist(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double, Arg4 As Double, Arg5 As Boolean) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the hypergeometric distribution. HypGeom_Dist returns the probability of a given number of sample successes, given the sample size, population successes, and population size. Use HypGeom_Dist for problems with a finite population, where each observation is either a success or a failure, and where each subset of a given size is chosen with equal likelihood.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Sample_s - the number of successes in the sample.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Number_sample - the size of the sample.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Population_s - the number of successes in the population.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Number_population - the population size.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Cumulative - a logical value that determines the form of the function. If cumulative is True, HypGeom_Dist returns the cumulative distribution function; if False, it returns the probability mass function.

members.name: HypGeomDist
members.signature: HypGeomDist(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double, Arg4 As Double) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the hypergeometric distribution. HypGeomDist returns the probability of a given number of sample successes, given the sample size, population successes, and population size. Use HypGeomDist for problems with a finite population, where each observation is either a success or a failure, and where each subset of a given size is chosen with equal likelihood.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Sample_s - the number of successes in the sample.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Number_sample - the size of the sample.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Population_s - the number of successes in the population.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Number_population - the population size.

members.name: IntRate
members.signature: IntRate(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant, Arg3 As Variant, Arg4 As Variant, [Arg5 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the interest rate for a fully invested security.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Settlement - the security's settlement date. The security settlement date is the date after the issue date when the security is traded to the buyer.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Maturity - the security's maturity date. The maturity date is the date when the security expires.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Investment - the amount invested in the security.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Redemption - the amount to be received at maturity.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Basis - the type of day count basis to use.

members.name: Ipmt
members.signature: Ipmt(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double, Arg4 As Double, [Arg5 As Variant], [Arg6 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the interest payment for a given period for an investment based on periodic, constant payments and a constant interest rate.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Rate - the interest rate per period.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Per - the period for which you want to find the interest; must be in the range 1 to nper.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Nper - the total number of payment periods in an annuity.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Pv - the present value, or the lump-sum amount that a series of future payments is worth right now.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Fv - the future value, or a cash balance that you want to attain after the last payment is made. If fv is omitted, it is assumed to be 0 (the future value of a loan, for example, is 0).

params.name: Arg6
params.text: Type - the number 0 or 1 and indicates when payments are due. If type is omitted, it is assumed to be 0.

members.name: Irr
members.signature: Irr(Arg1 As Variant, [Arg2 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the internal rate of return for a series of cash flows represented by the numbers in values. These cash flows don't have to be even, as they would be for an annuity. However, the cash flows must occur at regular intervals, such as monthly or annually. The internal rate of return is the interest rate received for an investment consisting of payments (negative values) and income (positive values) that occur at regular periods.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Values - an array or a reference to cells that contain numbers for which you want to calculate the internal rate of return.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Guess - a number that you guess is close to the result of Irr.

members.name: LinEst

doc.summary: Calculates the statistics for a line by using the least squares method to calculate a straight line that best fits your data, and returns an array that describes the line. Because this function returns an array of values, it must be entered as an array formula.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Known_y's - the set of y-values that you already know in the relationship y = mx + b.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Known_x's - an optional set of x-values that you may already know in the relationship y = mx + b.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Const - a logical value specifying whether to force the constant b to equal 0.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Stats - a logical value specifying whether to return additional regression statistics.

doc.summary: Returns the natural logarithm of a number. Natural logarithms are based on the constant e (2.71828182845904).

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the positive real number for which you want the natural logarithm.

members.signature: Log(Arg1 As Double, [Arg2 As Variant]) As Double

params.name: Arg2

doc.summary: Returns the base-10 logarithm of a number.

params.name: Arg1

members.name: LogEst
members.signature: LogEst(Arg1 As Variant, [Arg2 As Variant], [Arg3 As Variant], [Arg4 As Variant]) As Variant

doc.summary: In regression analysis, calculates an exponential curve that fits your data, and returns an array of values that describes the curve. Because this function returns an array of values, it must be entered as an array formula.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Known_y's - the set of y-values that you already know in the relationship y = b*m^x.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Known_x's - an optional set of x-values that you may already know in the relationship y = b*m^x.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Const - a logical value specifying whether to force the constant b to equal 1.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Stats - a logical value specifying whether to return additional regression statistics.

members.name: NegBinom_Dist
members.signature: NegBinom_Dist(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double, Arg4 As Boolean) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the negative binomial distribution. NegBinom_Dist returns the probability that there will be number_f failures before the number_s-th success, when the constant probability of a success is probability_s. This function is similar to the binomial distribution, except that the number of successes is fixed, and the number of trials is variable. Like the binomial, trials are assumed to be independent.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number_f - the number of failures.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Number_s - the threshold number of successes.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Probability_s - the probability of a success.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Cumulative - A logical value that determines the form of the function. If cumulative is True, NegBinom_Dist returns the cumulative distribution function; if False, it returns the probability density function.

members.name: NegBinomDist
members.signature: NegBinomDist(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the negative binomial distribution. NegBinomDist returns the probability that there will be number_f failures before the number_s-th success, when the constant probability of a success is probability_s. This function is similar to the binomial distribution, except that the number of successes is fixed, and the number of trials is variable. Like the binomial, trials are assumed to be independent.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number_f - the number of failures.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Number_s - the threshold number of successes.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Probability_s - the probability of a success.

members.name: NetworkDays
members.signature: NetworkDays(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant, [Arg3 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the number of whole working days between start_date and end_date. Working days exclude weekends and any dates identified in holidays. Use NetworkDays to calculate employee benefits that accrue based on the number of days worked during a specific term.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Start_date - a date that represents the start date.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: End_date - a date that represents the end date.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Holidays - an optional range of one or more dates to exclude from the working calendar, such as state and federal holidays and floating holidays. The list can be either a range of cells that contains the dates or an array constant of the serial numbers that represent the dates.

members.name: NetworkDays_Intl
members.signature: NetworkDays_Intl(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant, [Arg3 As Variant], [Arg4 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the number of whole workdays between two dates using parameters to indicate which and how many days are weekend days. Weekend days and any days that are specified as holidays are not considered as workdays.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Start_date - The start date for which the difference is to be computed. The start_date can be earlier than, the same as, or later than the end_date.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: End_date - The end date for which the difference is to be computed. The start_date can be earlier than, the same as, or later than the end_date.

params.name: Arg3
params.text:
Weekend - Indicates the days of the week that are weekend days and are not included in the number of whole working days between start_date and end_date. Weekend is a weekend number or string that specifies when weekends occur. Weekend number values indicate the weekend days listed in the following table.
Weekend numberWeekend days
1 or omittedSaturday, Sunday
2Sunday, Monday
3Monday, Tuesday
4Tuesday, Wednesday
5Wednesday, Thursday
6Thursday, Friday
7Friday, Saturday
11Sunday only
12Monday only
13Tuesday only
14Wednesday only
15Thursday only
16Friday only
17Saturday only

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Holidays - An optional set of one or more dates that are to be excluded from the working day calendar. Holidays is a range of cells that contain the dates, or an array constant of the serial values that represent those dates. The ordering of dates or serial values in holidays can be arbitrary.

members.name: NPer
members.signature: NPer(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double, [Arg4 As Variant], [Arg5 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the number of periods for an investment based on periodic, constant payments and a constant interest rate.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Rate - the interest rate per period.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Pmt - the payment made each period; it cannot change over the life of the annuity. Typically, pmt contains principal and interest but no other fees or taxes.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Pv - the present value, or the lump-sum amount that a series of future payments is worth right now.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Fv - the future value, or a cash balance that you want to attain after the last payment is made. If fv is omitted, it is assumed to be 0 (the future value of a loan, for example, is 0).

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Type - the number 0 or 1 and indicates when payments are due.

members.name: Oct2Bin
members.signature: Oct2Bin(Arg1 As Variant, [Arg2 As Variant]) As String

doc.summary: Converts an octal number to binary.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the octal number that you want to convert. Number may not contain more than 10 characters. The most significant bit of number is the sign bit. The remaining 29 bits are magnitude bits. Negative numbers are represented using two's-complement notation.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Places - the number of characters to use. If places is omitted, Oct2Bin uses the minimum number of characters necessary. Places is useful for padding the return value with leading 0s (zeros).

doc.summary: Converts an octal number to decimal.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - the octal number that you want to convert. Number may not contain more than 10 octal characters (30 bits). The most significant bit of number is the sign bit. The remaining 29 bits are magnitude bits. Negative numbers are represented using two's-complement notation.

doc.summary: Converts an octal number to hexadecimal.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Places - the number of characters to use. If places is omitted, Oct2Hex uses the minimum number of characters necessary. Places is useful for padding the return value with leading 0s (zeros).

members.name: Percentile

members.name: PercentRank
members.signature: PercentRank(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Double, [Arg3 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the rank of a value in a data set as a percentage of the data set. This function can be used to evaluate the relative standing of a value within a data set. For example, you can use PercentRank to evaluate the standing of an aptitude test score among all scores for the test.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Array - the array or range of data with numeric values that defines relative standing.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: X - the value for which you want to know the rank.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Significance - an optional value that identifies the number of significant digits for the returned percentage value. If omitted, PercentRank uses three digits (0.xxx).

members.name: PercentRank_Exc
members.signature: PercentRank_Exc(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Double, [Arg3 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the rank of a value in a data set as a percentage (0..1, exclusive) of the data set.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Array - The array or range of data with numeric values that defines relative standing.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: X - The value for which you want to know the rank.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Significance - A value that identifies the number of significant digits for the returned percentage value. If omitted, PercentRank_Exc uses three digits (0.xxx).

members.name: PercentRank_Inc
members.signature: PercentRank_Inc(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Double, [Arg3 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the rank of a value in a data set as a percentage (0..1, inclusive) of the data set. This function can be used to evaluate the relative standing of a value within a data set. For example, you can use PercentRank_Inc to evaluate the standing of an aptitude test score among all scores for the test.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Array - The array or range of data with numeric values that defines relative standing.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: X - The value for which you want to know the rank.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Significance - An optional value that identifies the number of significant digits for the returned percentage value. If omitted, PercentRank_Inc uses three digits (0.xxx).

members.name: Permut
members.signature: Permut(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the number of permutations for a given number of objects that can be selected from number objects. A permutation is any set or subset of objects or events where internal order is significant. Permutations are different from combinations, for which the internal order is not significant. Use this function for lottery-style probability calculations.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Number - an integer that describes the number of objects.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Number_chosen - an integer that describes the number of objects in each permutation.

members.name: Pmt
members.signature: Pmt(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Double, [Arg4 As Variant], [Arg5 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Calculates the payment for a loan based on constant payments and a constant interest rate.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: Rate - the interest rate for the loan.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Nper - the total number of payments for the loan.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Pv - the present value, or the total amount that a series of future payments is worth now; also known as the principal.

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Fv - the future value, or a cash balance you want to attain after the last payment is made. If fv is omitted, it is assumed to be 0 (zero), that is, the future value of a loan is 0.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Type - the number 0 (zero) or 1; indicates when payments are due.

members.name: Poisson
members.signature: Poisson(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Boolean) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the Poisson distribution. A common application of the Poisson distribution is predicting the number of events over a specific time, such as the number of cars arriving at a toll plaza in 1 minute.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: X - the number of events.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Mean - the expected numeric value.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Cumulative - a logical value that determines the form of the probability distribution returned. If cumulative is True, Poisson returns the cumulative Poisson probability that the number of random events occurring will be between zero and x inclusive; if False, it returns the Poisson probability mass function that the number of events occurring will be exactly x.

members.name: Poisson_Dist
members.signature: Poisson_Dist(Arg1 As Double, Arg2 As Double, Arg3 As Boolean) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the Poisson distribution. A common application of the Poisson distribution is predicting the number of events over a specific time, such as the number of cars arriving at a toll plaza in one minute.

params.name: Arg1
params.text: X - The number of events.

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Mean - The expected numeric value.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Cumulative - A logical value that determines the form of the probability distribution returned. If cumulative is True, Poisson_Dist returns the cumulative Poisson probability that the number of random events occurring will be between 0 (zero) and x inclusive; if False, it returns the Poisson probability mass function that the number of events occurring will be exactly x.

members.name: Ppmt

params.name: Arg4
params.text: Pv - the present value—the total amount that a series of future payments is worth now.

params.name: Arg5
params.text: Fv - the future value, or a cash balance you want to attain after the last payment is made. If fv is omitted, it is assumed to be 0 (zero), that is, the future value of a loan is 0.

params.name: Arg6
params.text: Type - the number 0 or 1 and indicates when payments are due.

members.name: Price
members.signature: Price(Arg1 As Variant, Arg2 As Variant, Arg3 As Variant, Arg4 As Variant, Arg5 As Variant, Arg6 As Variant, [Arg7 As Variant]) As Double

doc.summary: Returns the price per $100 face value of a security that pays periodic interest.

params.name: Arg1

params.name: Arg2
params.text: Maturity - the security's maturity date. The maturity date is the date when the security expires.

params.name: Arg3
params.text: Rate - the security's annual coupon rate.

params.name: Arg4

params.name: Arg5

params.name: Arg6
params.text: Frequency - the number of coupon payments per year. For annual payments, frequency = 1; for semiannual, frequency = 2; for quarterly, frequency = 4.

params.name: Arg7
params.text: Basis - the type of day count basis to use.

params.name: Arg3
